topic: Toronto winter rant — too cold for a Jamaican

turns.content: I know it's almost June but I need to RANT about this past winter because I am still TRAUMATIZED. Toronto winter is NOT for people who grew up in warm weather!! I swear my bones don't understand cold!!
Ha! You've been holding that in for a while clearly. Toronto winters are brutal even for people who grew up here. What pushed you over the edge this year?

there was that week in February where it hit like -28 with the windchill!! MINUS TWENTY EIGHT!! that is not a temperature that should exist for humans!! I had to walk from the streetcar to the venue and I thought my face was going to fall off. literally could not feel my cheeks for like 20 minutes!!

-28 windchill is genuinely dangerous territory. That's the kind of cold where exposed skin can get frostbite in under 10 minutes. Were you properly layered up or were you doing the classic "I'm not giving up my outfit for warmth" thing?

ok LISTEN. I had on a puffer jacket but I refuse to wear those big ugly snow boots to a set!! I had my regular shoes on and my toes went NUMB. like fully numb. I couldn't even feel the bass through the floor for the first hour which is a TRAGEDY for a DJ!!

Not being able to feel the bass -- that IS a tragedy. But real talk, you've gotta protect your feet in that kind of cold. Here's the move a lot of people do: wear the warm boots for the commute and pack your set shoes in your bag. Change when you get to the venue. No shame in it -- your toes will thank you.

Also, thermal insoles are a game changer. You can get heated insoles that are USB rechargeable for like $30-40. Slip them in your shoes and they keep your feet warm for 3-5 hours.

heated insoles are a TING?? ok that might save my life next winter. because I'm NOT wearing snow boots to the venue that kills the whole vibe!! but I also can't be doing sets with frozen feet that's crazy lol. and don't even get me started on carrying equipment in the cold — trying to load gear when your fingers can't grip anything!!

Loading gear in the cold is the worst. Cold fingers plus heavy equipment plus icy sidewalks is a dangerous combination. A few things that help:

- **Touchscreen-compatible gloves** so you can still use your phone without taking them off. Some are thin enough that you can still grip cables and connectors.
- **Hand warmers** -- those little packets you shake and they heat up for 8 hours. Keep a few in your pockets and your gear bag.
- **Do two trips instead of one** -- better to make an extra trip than to overload yourself and slip on ice carrying a mixer.

I slipped on ice TWICE this winter!! once carrying a bag of cables and once just walking to get coffee. both times my whole life flashed before my eyes lol. where I'm from we don't have ICE on the ground!! that concept is FOREIGN to me!! the ground should not be trying to KILL you!!

Hahaha "the ground should not be trying to kill you" -- honestly that should be on a t-shirt. Ice walking is a genuine skill that takes years to develop. Here are the cold weather immigrant survival tips:

- **Walk like a penguin** on ice. Short steps, feet pointed slightly outward, keep your center of gravity over your front leg. It looks silly but it works.
- **Yaktrax or ice cleats** -- they slip over your shoes and have little metal coils or spikes on the bottom. They grip ice like nothing else. About $25 at Canadian Tire.
- **Avoid the shiny patches** -- if the sidewalk looks wet and glossy in sub-zero temps, it's not wet, it's black ice. Walk on the snowy part instead, more traction.

WALK LIKE A PENGUIN lmaooo!! I'm dead!! but honestly at -28 I will try ANYTHING. and the thing is I LOVE Toronto in the summer. like June to September this city is INCREDIBLE. the energy, the festivals, the patios. but November through March?? this city is trying to END me. 5 months of suffering for 4 months of paradise!!

That's the Toronto bargain and everyone who lives here has accepted it on some level. The summers really are world-class though -- the city completely transforms. Some winter survival strategies beyond gear:

- **Vitamin D supplements** -- seriously. The lack of sunlight from November to March tanks your energy and mood. A lot of doctors in Toronto recommend 1000-2000 IU daily through winter.
- **SAD lamp** -- a light therapy lamp for 20-30 minutes in the morning can make a huge difference. The darkness at 4:30pm is as hard as the cold for a lot of people.
- **Find indoor things you love** -- a lot of Torontonians treat winter as studio season, gym season, cooking season. Lean into the hibernation instead of fighting it.

studio season!! ok THAT I can do. winter is when I get the most production done because there's literally nothing else to do lol. and yeah the darkness at 4:30pm is EVIL. going to the studio in the dark and leaving in the dark?? my body doesn't know what time it is for 5 months straight. but you know what I'm already feeling better just because the sun is out again. summer is almost here and I will SURVIVE another year!! warm weather people are built TOUGH for enduring this!!

You ARE tough for enduring it -- a lot of people from warm climates can't hack it and leave after one or two winters. The fact that you're still here and thriving says a lot. And hey, all those winter studio hours probably made you a better producer, so the cold is paying dividends even if it doesn't feel like it in February. Enjoy this summer -- you've earned it!
